entry.title: A "Quantum-Agile" Bitcoin address proposal
entry.summary:
The discussion highlights the complexities and challenges surrounding the adoption of Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) within Bitcoin's structure. The integration of multiple cryptographic schemes into Bitcoin does not necessarily equate to increased cryptographic agility but can be seen as a response to the inherent limitations and trade-offs involved with current PQC technologies. These include large signature sizes, high validation costs, and a lack of advanced features such as homomorphic derivation. The necessity to support multiple cryptographic schemes arises from the unlikelihood of a single PQC scheme fulfilling all security needs efficiently.
In the context of Bitcoin, unlike protocols like TLS/SSH/IPSec, the focus extends beyond protecting individual transactions or holdings. There is a communal imperative to secure the network to protect the value of Bitcoin as a whole against potential threats like large-scale thefts, which could impact all users’ assets. This collective responsibility suggests that every user has a stake in ensuring the overall security of the ecosystem, akin to a tragedy of the commons where personal incentives align with communal security.
Moreover, the implementation of multiple PQC schemes could potentially slow down the decision-making process within the Bitcoin community, as consensus would need to be reached on the security of each new scheme introduced. This extensive consensus requirement makes Bitcoin one of the least agile cryptographic systems. Although flexibility in choosing between multiple PQC schemes seems beneficial, it predominantly falls on wallet providers and system implementers rather than end-users, limiting the practical autonomy of users to transition to newer, possibly more secure cryptographic methods.
This discourse also touches on recent developments in stateful and stateless hash-based signature schemes, acknowledging their significant trade-offs concerning cost and security depending on specific use cases. Despite these advancements, the integration of such technologies into Bitcoin’s framework remains an intricate challenge marked by necessary compromises rather than ideal solutions.
